**Title: Trip Report - Marshalship Drive - Bryan - Al Faqa’a**

Trip Report - Marshalship Drive - Bryan - Al Faqa’a
									Sept 25, 2023 17:59:54 GMT 4
									
									Rey, LOWELL, and  3 more  like this
								
								
									Quote
									Edit
									
									Select PostDeselect PostLink to PostReport PostMemberSend MessageModerateDelete PostMove PostOnly This PostAnd All AboveAnd All BelowAnd All SelectedWarn MemberIP 2001:8f8:1b27:3091:912bThreadsPostsMembersBack to Top
								
							
							Post by Bryan on Sept 25, 2023 17:59:54 GMT 4
							Trip Report - Marshalship Drive - Bryan - Al Faqa’a 24 September 2023Drive Difficulty: Newbie/RookieDETAILS:Date: Sunday, 24 Sep 2023Meeting Time: 6:15 AMMoving Time: 6:30 AMDrive End: Before 10AMLocation: Al Faqa’aMeeting Point: (24.6504167, 55.5281389)Google Maps: maps.app.goo.gl/aCdnQyRazLRjutfc7Deflation Point: 24.65002, 55.52400This is a spacial drive for my marshalship so I am excited and nervous at the same time.As soon as I finished packing all my gears (made sure I had complete recovery gears including Jack and boards since Im leading a Newbie convoy.) I arrived with my brother at Emarat- Grayteesah at 8pm where our rendezvous point was for everyone who were camping before the drive. Francis, Jon, Rholy, were there already. we waited for Javee, Jess and Marvin while we refuelled and bought some drinks and ice. I started being lead from the meeting point. As soon as the rest arrived, we agreed on the radio frequency and moved out shortly after.Drove the convoy to the deflation point by the fence in Al Faqa’a where we deflated and installed our flags. From here, I have decided on the convoy arrangement going to the camp site. We decided to go to the usual COMPASS Anniversary camp site, it was just over 2 kilometers drive. As soon as we arrived at the usual camp site, we decided to find a better place as the usual one had lots of bushes and it was not as flat as it was last year.We started setting up the common area, the tables and chairs then started cooking while we watched a movie on a big projector screen. Just before the movie got over, the rest of the campers have arrived they were total 9 cars, all of them brought their share of food and drinks.. We had so much food and so much fun.. as soon as the movie ended, I did set up the karaoke then the real party started.. we all did our turns on the microphone singing, shouting, making jokes. I was one of the last ones to sleep as I had to turn off and pack the karaoke systems etc, that was around 3:30am.I was the first one to wake up at 5:45 am. I gradually packed my bed, chairs and secured all my gears in the car. Jason woke up, I then asked him to pick up Mark who is one our new drivers along with others. Marvin also woke up and started packing his gears. Then everyone woke up 1 by 1 and started packing while the others made coffee.I had to take another medicine for my headache, I was lucky I felt better just few minutes later.Before my briefing, I had asked everyone to reset their Tyre PSIs at 10psi I gathered all the members (4 Newbies, 5 Rookies, 6 Intermediates and 4 Advance drivers) A lot ehh??So I started my briefing with a quick introduction and ask everyone around to quickly introduce themselves. There is so much to cover and so much to discuss during the briefing but at the same time you would want to make it as short as possible as you don’t want to bore the drivers and you don’t want to take so much time which will affect the planned end time of the drive.During my briefing, I have discussed the following:•	How COMPASS was founded•	The learning Rubrics of COMPASS – explained how to progress from Newbie, to Rookie, Intermediate and Advance Rank•	The objective of Compass as to why we consider each drive as Training drive while we still try to have fun. How we make sure the safety of everyone•	I have discussed the basic tools required in safe offroading and mentioned that the same requirements needs to be completed before becoming a Rookie:    o	Tyre Pressure gauge    o	Compressor    o	Flag – explained importance of flag and reading how it whips    o	Two-way Radio – explained why its important and correct way of communicating, avoiding unnecessary discussions, Listening carefully on the instructions from Me and the supports    o	Alligator Jack with minimum 2 Tons capacity    o	Jack board    o	Kinetic Rope – explained why kinetic Rope and its advantages    o	Soft Shackles    o	Tow points – explained risks if these are not in good condition    o	Shovel    o	Spare Tyre and the tools to remove and replace – explained how important this is to keep the type in good condition.. you don’t need it until you actually need it    o	Fire Extinguisher – forgot to mention this but it should be in here for reference as well•	Explained the removal of the ABS/Traction Control fuse or installing a kill switch for it•	Discussed the importance of following the convoy / convoy management where I have also assigned the drivers position as well as my supports    o	Mark being first timer, followed by Marlon, then Kiko and finally Rholy for the newbie. Following the newbie convoy were the Rookies, Ferds, Neil, Jeff, Ronald, Ranz.    o	Supports on the left floating were Jon and Javee, on the right floating were Jason and Pongs, and last car and Sweep was Vong (Thank you guys a lot for the complete support during my drive)    o	Always follow the track – explained why this is important and what could potentially happen if you don’t    o	Maintaining 3-4 cars distance    o	Observing the car infront of you at all times    o	No fighting gravity – very important. If you do fight, Gravity always wins    o	Always stop on a safe position, nose down or bum down    o	Wait until the previous driver is clear before attempting•	Explained the science of pop outs and how to avoid it•	Importance of me knowing that everyone is comfortable during the drive and letting me know when they are not•	During Self recovery or when stuck to wait for my instruction (or from the support) to avoid accidents – correct way of pointing the steering wheel and position of the car where reversing from a due•	No hard braking and why•	Explained that re attempt is better than overshooting – explained that we are all here to learn and we all started from somewhere.. don’t feel bad when getting stuck or re-trying a maneuver Though the terrain wasn’t as I expected (filled of bushes,, literally 1 foot apart from each other) we had fun during the drive and Im sure each drivers learned one or two points here and there.At some point I had to split the convoy Marvin was kind enough to lead the Rookie halfway to the exitI had to deviate a little from my original planned route considering the time and one car had some sound coming from the axle. In the end of the day as we learn have fun, we also want to make sure that our cars in good condition after the driveI have chosen to exit by the Solar tower under some trees just to have some shade while inflatingI have reminded the drivers to dis engage 4-wheel drives, un install the flags etcI have also asked the drivers to visit the Forum site where they can give us our feedback on the drive. Each drive are unique, experience varies and ultimately knowing the feedback from drivers gives us opportunity to improve the succeeding drives and Compass group in general. It also feels good as the lead of the drive when drivers share their learnings and helps other future drivers as well (Trip Report)The drive ended before 11am, a little bit longer than the planned 10am exit.All in all out ( all drivers and passengers were safe, all cars were intact)I have learned:•	You need to keep on guiding and talking to the drivers, this was not easy considering the tricky terrain and bushes around. talking to them constantly makes them feel comfortable and engaged.•	Leading a convoy specially this long is very tricky. Making sure you consider all drivers and all supports. In the end no matter how complicated the situation is.. you just have to remember few things; You can do it! You are trained to do so ; Keep them safe ; don’t leave anyone behind.•	Ego is one thing that you should not bring with you during the drive. Listening and considering each Marshals advise.. They want you to be better than them, so what ever they tell you is solely for you. This goes to the drivers as well, when marshals and supports tells you something, it is not to show that they are better than you in any way. They are trying to pass on what they know and they are making sure you are all safe•	There are so much to learn, even when I become a marshal (if ever I passed). There is no limit in learning and sharing the sameThank you, Marvin, for guiding and teaching me through my journey of becoming a Marshal.Thank you Reswee, Mako, Francis and Joseph for your continuous teachings and advises. I wouldn't be where I am now (in Offroading) if it weren’t for you.To all who volunteered to support me,, masakit na kamay ko Hindi ko na itytype isa isa pangalan nyo.. Thank you very much!Lastly, many thanks to our founding Marshals in establishing the group COMPASS the only group I have joined and stayed. Please be patient with me as I continue to learn.Keep the sands flying!

							Post by mike1976 on Sept 18, 2023 16:59:59 GMT 4
							Bago ako magsimula nais ko munang pasalamatan lagi ang mga Marshalls for all of their generosity of sharing their skills, time and sacrifices. and immediately I want to quote what Marshall Mako said in the temp CG all level."I don’t play in the desert much anymore, so expect me to be excited and ready to kill ourselves. If you roll over, make sure to turn off car engine right away. If you die … don’t worry, you only die once." Hindi talaga sya nag jo-joke. Running 70-90 kph in the dunes feeling nya nasa sheikh Zayed kami. Hahahaha. It was Sep.16 starting  time is 630am but Marshall Mako arrive at 645 am expecting us that we are ready. 715 am he told us we will check psi climbing some dunes. Pero hindi totoo budol yung check nang psi. Because he is soo exited the approach is it is up close your eyes and pray. And its not yet warm up sabi nya. hahahhaha Then we move to the real drive that we never ever catch him the gap is always 10 dunes or 2 klm. The difference between rookie and intermediate is 10x faster at kung hindi mo talaga natutunan yung proper training from newbie to rookie lalong lalo ka hindi makaka sabay sa intermediate. it is fast pacing. Kaya ang mahirap kapag naiwanan ka nang lead hindi mo makikita ang movement niya at hindi nya rin sasabihin ang mga bulaga at kaldag moment.hahahahha. The intermediate is not for the fainted heart but with the proper guidance and training, intermediate is the beggining of fun and highest adrenalin. We start at 730 from the camp we arrive in Marshall Randy camp in Nakra at 915 and Marshall Mako said we already cover the whole drive route that how fast we are. then we lunch there until we start again at 130 pm. And this time i said to my self hindi talaga ako magpapaiwan lipad kung lipad i dont care dahil kapag naiwanan ka ang hirap makahabol dahil sa sa level na ito they will not wait for you. You need to follow. and this second part going back to starting point is the extremist drive that i did in my off road life. This is the drive that literaly 60 -70 kph side slopping, cresting,switching in 45 degree and up,up,up close your eyes and pray. hahahahhaha. Lesson learn:To invest in a good shock absorberTo have a good clearanceYou must really, really know your car capacity and skillsYou must really, really know the proper skills of manuever and execution that we learn from newbie and rookie.Rookie to intermediate is 10 times speed.Rookie is fun but Intermediate is funner. hahahahhahahhaOnce again I salute and thanking the Marshalls for your all sacrifice of time to share your joy and skill sa akin. Special thanks to Marshall Mako and Marshall Marvin and bro Bryan to support us with this Intermediate introductory. But most infortant I praise God the creator of all things who let us enjoy Him and His creation at iningatan tayo lahat. all of this is for His glory alone. God bless you all. Sa muli nating pagkikita.

**Title: TRIP REPORT: Dark Flag Catcher Track (Night Drive)**

TRIP REPORT: Dark Flag Catcher Track (Night Drive)

							Post by MangAriesyo on Sept 15, 2019 8:25:44 GMT 4
							Lead Marshal: JcSweep: ResweeDrivers: 1. Aries2. Luis3. Kiko4. ArchieI was the first one to arrived in Kalba meeting point. After a few minutes Marshal Jc, Luis, Archie and Kiko came. We all came exactly on our agreed meeting time. This is the difference between Rookie and Intermediate. Rookie, the convoy will wait for you if you are late, but in Intermediate, if your late, you have to catch the convoy on your own.  That’s how fun and strict the Intermediate level.Had our short briefing about the track that we have to take. This night drive will take the Flag Catch Route from Kalba Road to Badayer. We are going to trail the following sites:    Fossil Rock    Camel Rock    Devil's Plunge (a bit further due to dug route, and its accident record)    2nd December (stop point)    Pink Rock    Big Red    Badayer Shop (END)We started with a few hiccups, nabalahaw agad ako. Hindi pa kasi umiinit. Then after a few minutes we roll like rockstar with a fast pace drive.  In this drive, it’s a complete radio silence. Why? Because you need to focus on the drive and distance between the convoy. Atleast 2-4 cars apart as we are tackling a high dunes. You only use the radio when you take a second try or for recovery.  The convoy doesn’t stop when you are stuck, you have to do self recovery if it is possible then follow the convoy. Inform the marshal that you are going to take a second try and when connecting to the convoy. That is why it is important to have enough lights so you can follow the track and of course your gps.We usually changes the car arrangement in the convoy along the way. When we arrived in the first stop, we did a 5 minutes interval then proceed again. When we reached 2nd December point, Marshal Reswee joined us.We camped in Pink Rock and had our sumptuous dinner, Papaitan, Sizzling Chicken, Deep Fried Chicken, Fish Fillet and Pancit. Had some beers and chitchat, then we hit the sack around 3am.We have to leave the camp as early as 6am to have out private drive and meet Mando to join us in Badayer shops.We finished our drive around 9:30am.Indeed har a well spent Thursday Night Drive with the rockstarsBig Thanks for Marshal Jc and Reswee and to all drivers!Keep the sands flying!COMPASS for life!#yotaforlife#fortuner#compass

**Title: Trip Report: Al Khatim to Sweihan Traverse IV**

							Post by pittoy on Nov 18, 2018 20:37:43 GMT 4
							Rookie Drive (Rookies in Naqra 2)SweihanLead Marshal: RedbeastSupport Marshalls: Reswee and MakoThe night started on the 1st Anniv of our allied Pwr52 offroader. It was a busy night for me as I am the one being assigned in the mentioned group to be the Guest transport from the main road bringing them to the camp. The night was fun, full of friends from other Filipino groups. But important is my self discipline not to have sleepless night as it will be a long technical drive on the next morning.08:30hrs we started our drive with the routine safety briefing at AlKhatim then moving towards the crocodile lake and bedford truck. As we goes by more technical driving has been encounters and introduced.Lesson Learned: 1. Tip on Straight crestin (feeling the front of your car goes up and having quick look of the sidemirror level to the ridge on top of the dunes - control of speed / no flying). 2. Tip on side cresting (small and big crest are all the same, its just a matter of how to approach it correctly/properly, approach will to be aligned and let it sit to the crest and not exiting quickly. momentum will cause rollover when exiting too early from the crest)3. Leftfoot braking Technique - very useful for self recovery when mastered properly. It helps me self recover when I was stuck in the ridge of the dunes and 70% of my jeep is on the steep RHS of the dune, when recovery is almost impossible on that position. As instructed by Marshall Mako, I managed to do the left foot brake, 1500RPM then slowly releasing the brake and keep on pumping slowly the gas, steering on my full right and fortunately a safe/ successful self-recovery down to a safe ground.4. Better to go for how many tries than to have a one roll-over5. Exiting a big bowl - let your car gain the momentum doing in a circular motion around the bowl until you reach to the top to exit the bowl.6. Always listen to the Lead, and to the marshalls7. Second lead and Sweeper plays a big role as well. Second lead - corrects some tracks of the lead, giving additional safety precautions and instructions to the group. Sweeper - gives / relays feedback to the lead. Gives total count of the convoy and reports to the lead if needs to stop, slow, adjust, catch up the convoy management. Also gives feedback to the lead if someone is stuck and convoy needs to stop. Gives feedback to the lead if all cars are moving in the convoy..8. Take into consideration- 11-12 mid noon, the contrast of the sand is very low, and visibility is going now to be low. The long drive finished around 16:00hrs inspite of Neil's transmission clutch burnt, we still managed to return to the main road safe and sound.And completed the day with yummy chicken/ mutton mandi , smiles in our faces, stress free being and a happy Compass..AlKHatim to Sweihan 8hrs dirve was a fullfillment.. A big thanks to the Lead Randy and supporting Marshalls Reswee and Mako.WeARECompass!!

							A traverse drive from Madinat Zayed to Arada and Moreeb.Drive Difficulty: RookieDrive Details:Date: February 11-12-13, 2022Meeting Time: 9:30PM, Feb 11 Friday.Moving Time: 10PMDubaiMeeting Point: Last Exit, Abu Dhabi Bound Coordinates: goo.gl/maps/qicr7FUpnqVJce98A24°54'49.5"N 54°57'39.3"E24.913735, 54.960923Marshals:FrancisResweeDonMCWe met at Last exit Jebel Ali Abu Dhabi bound ( I was not late! ). Such a long drive from that meeting point till we reached Deflation point, just few minutes from the camp site. we reached at the camp site just around 2am I think where we started with funny stories while Javee was cooking Sopas Lights off at 4am to prepare for a long day drive in the morning. Call time was 8am. Had a quick noodles in the morning, packed my camping stuff and secured all loose items.Briefing with the Marshals about Liwa and general, what to expect.. what to remember, what to avoid etc. discussed about safety distance between cars, what kind of drives we were gonna have, convoy positions, recovering safely, and many. more.First few minutes of the drive, my hands were sweating. combination of excitement and boy I was anxious! then I started feeling just pure fun and excitement. I have been to Liwa twice and this is the first time I have really enjoyed the "real" liwa dunes. my previous trips we just pure over-landing and camping experiences. We stopped at out first highlight destination point which was the Yellow Truck (which some were expecting an actual yellow coloured Track    ) This is where we also camped for Lunch, Thanks to Don and Francis for the yummy lunch. I think it was before 4pm when we started driving again towards Arada if Im not mistaken. Beautiful dunes everywhere! just before reaching the exit, "pwede po ba ko muna mag poopoo?" a calm voice like a kid from the radio and everyone started cracking!  . When we reached the next destination, this is were some of the drivers headed home due to some commitments next day.. A quick drive from here to the next camp site where we stayed for the night. Don as usual and his lovely wife prepared some yummy soup and beefy veggie dinner which everyone enjoyed along with some steamed rice, all freshly cooked. again some nice stories and laughter. Lights off at around 1 am to prepare for next morning drive.Next morning, Cup noodles again and some coffee. packed all stuff, secured and got ready for the drive. as usual, the briefing with the marshals, things to remember before the drive. again, Liwa kept surprising me with these tall clean dunes. each climbs were different. Ended the drive at Moreeb dunes.. I had quite a blast here trying to climb the tall wall. All in All out!Points I have learned:Straight crest, but this is way different from previous experience, these are TALL dunes! - how to safely climb the dunes with the right power and right momentum, also very important how to go down from them safely avoiding fish tail scenario or event rollovers.Side slopes, cresting and switching,  again way different and challenging. Maintaining proper distancing as you want to have that right distance and momentum to climb as well as proper positioning if someone had to do a retry.proper recovery during stuckradio discipline (may sound silly, yet very important)and many many moreThanks to the Marshals who are tirelessly arranging all these events and drives. Thanks to the Chefs preparing yummy foods!, and thanks to everyone for the great sand time.--

							I left Abu Dhabi around 9 p.m., and went directly to the meeting point, but Lowell messaged that they were at Marshal Reswee's house. So I decided to follow them and meet other members at their house.  Around 12:40 a.m., we proceeded to the camping location. To my surprise, there was a police checkpoint in the area before Last Exit. I was a little bit worried since my registration had expired 4 days ago.We arrived at the camp site and had a small chit-chat, then Marshal Marvin briefed us around 2 a.m. for the GPS challenge way points .I woke up around 5 a.m., then at 5:20 a.m. I told Marshal that I would start early so I could finish immediately since it was going to be hot. Unfortunately, it did not go as planned. On the way to the first way point, I got ditched, and hell, I thought I would not be able to finish the exam. I dug for about an hour and then placed my maxtrax. Luckily, the car went up, and I was relieved. When I almost reached waypoint 1, my rear tire popped out, and at that time in my mind, "This is it.", its not my time. I have to give up, It's been 2 hours I've tried to fix it, but unfortunately it's not possible because I drove the car for a while before I noticed it pop out. After so many attempts and trying to fix it still failed, and everyone is giving me moral support   , they advised me to change the tire with the spare, and I got to move, and I was very exhausted.I proceeded to finish the balance way points, and it took me 40 minutes before I reached the last way point.Such an encounter is unforgettable., I realized that if I hadn't learned things in previous drives, I might not have been able to do proper digging technique or fix the pop out tire eventhough i did not succeed, but at least now I know how to assess those things if I ever find myself in a similar situation in the near future   .- Bring proper recovery tools.- Assess the dune before attacking it, and don’t be aggressive.- Keep your composure and try not to panic.Lastly, thanks to our marshals, Marvin, Resewee, and Mako, and also to Bryan and Bong to my co-examiners.

In this drive, finally my trans was replaced and fixed. Seems I adopt driving with a trans problem with high RPM, I did trials to adjust my driving with normal RPM and gearing. I got stuck 3 times but safely recovered especially on top of the crest with the kindness of 2 Marshall’s Reswee and Mako. But later I got rid of it and I slowly adjusted while we drive in egg baskets but still more practice in high hills and straight crest with a right momentum, gearing, and RPM. The problem didn’t finish being stuck, another problem comes we’re my right tyre at the back was cut after all we help some dudes who ask help for their 2 jeeps stuck for 2 hours when they try to recover each other. It happens when we’re almost going to the final stop in Al Qudra just after our Iftar break and great conversations in a nice weather. With the help of each other we replace my tyre, after Mako and Reswee help the guys to go out of the road and rescue 2 of their friends too who are lost. Lesson learned:* Gearing and RPM sometimes confusing to get the right momentum especially in the high hills and straight crest. Need to adjust and practice that situation in normal transmission from now. * Spare tyre? Yah it’s ridiculous if you don’t have one. It’s totally important being off-roader. You don’t know what’s next after all. * S.O.S.? Always pay attention and put your jeep in safe parking before giving help.* Water? It’s a must really. The guys we help are trying to recover their jeeps for almost 2 hours without enough water. 							Saturday: Afternoon DriveDate: 30 April 2022Meeting Time: 3:30 PMMeeting Point: EMARAT Petrol Station This is my first official Newbie drive.We entered the gates to the Al Faqa desert, and stopped at the entrance, where Marshal Reswee discussed the offroad driving basics, as well as the rules for driving in his convoy. After the briefing, we proceeded to tackle two main technics - straight crests and riding the slopes. Straight crests were a bit challenging at first as it required good judgement of speed and gas pedal control as I ascend towards the crests. The marshal carefully guided me step by step to get through the more challenging crests. I took a few tries for me to get used to this technique. Slope riding - seeing the slopes the first time was pretty intimidating for me, can't lie. LOL. My passenger asked me to just pass up on it coz they were a uneasy about it, so I radioed in to skip this one. Res and Rim insisted I should give it a try, even just on the lower portion of the slope which I did after they demonstrated me the technique. The sense of achievement encouraged me to go higher the next try, and then higher. And I did it, and boosted my confidence. So the trick I learned is to go full throttle going up and follow the shape of the dune. As you reach the top, just turn the nose back down and carefully glide the car. If the gravity starts to pull midway during the climb, then just turn and point the nose down early while still applying controlled power. I had fun learning the slopes (as well as self recovery.) By the end of the drive (approx 19:45), I could tell that my approach to ascends and descends have quite improved and I completed the drive without damaging the car lol. All big thanks to Reswee and Rim's support. My fellow drivers did amazing job going through the dunes, I learned from them as well while watching their movements. Overall, it was a great experience for me and I learned a lot. Looking forward to the next drive. I probably need a lift kit and bash plate hehe. Salamats ulit Res, Rim, Compass. Mabuhay!

Yellow/amber lights are useful in driving in gatch roads as white light does not penetrate the dust.• Having a standard mechanic’s set of tools for easy DIY troubleshooting is helpful to have in your vehicle.• Momentum & reflex is a key in tackling egg baskets. Always be mindful of the dunes around you so in case the vehicle in front of you stopped – you can safely maneuver to a safe position & avoid getting stucked.• In switch maneuvers, make sure to position your vehicle parallel to the top of the crest before switching.Overall, it was a pleasant & unique experience. Big thanks as always to Arjiel & Compass for organizing this drive & of course to my fellow drivers, specially to Reynan who relieve us from stress with his funny stories & anecdotes!

Once tail down or nose down. Steer to the where gravity is.- Left Right Wiggle! your steering wheel to get traction on straight crest maneuver.- Commit on maintaining RPM on uphill maneuver, once the slope passed you side mirrors let gravity do the work for you.- Gauge your terrain in front of your driver side mirror when you do not see any terrain in front of you. It will give you more confidence on where are you going and what does the terrain look like, just be aware the distance of the car ahead of you.- Gas up on side crest exit!TOOL CHECK DOOONNNEE! Salamat Marshal Francis for the Lead and Support Marshal JC, Don Rex and Len! Kita kits sa sunod sa drive!

							Date: 7th December 2019Drive Difficulty: 1 starMeeting Time: 05:30 AMMoving Time: 06:00 AMMeeting Point: goo.gl/maps/cnocf4WEeKN6AvRf6Lead Marshal: MakoNewbies:Willys RevillameMike P.This is my 3rd newbie drive.I’ve heard good things about Sweihan so I did not let the opportunity slip. As a noob driver, ito yung tipong di mo malilimutan at dahil first time ko din dun. Coming from Sharjah, the long trip was well worth it. We did not do a lot of straight crests. Instead, bro Mako focused on side crests para sulit talaga ang punta dun. Bro Mako lead the noob convoy, while bro Reswee lead the rookies.Sa safety briefing palang, I learned a lot of things already from the two marshals on top of the usual stuff. Like  the difference between side crest and side slope maneuvers and and its importance/application; how to deal with situations where gravity starts pulling you down during a side slope and you might hit a ditch or a tree on your way down; techiques sa mga steep climbs like zigzagging and sling shoting; how to steer the wheel when driving atop dune crests. They also shared some good techniques or insights on how leads manage to monitor what is happening with the convoy and how they find good tracks suitable for the convoy. As a newbie, these things inspire you to hone your skills sa drives and let you set your future goals so naappreciate ko yung mga nai-share nila – salamat!Fast paced yung drive so masaya! There were a lot of repetitions of maneuvers para masanay kami, left hand side sloping, right hand side sloping, sprinting. Matataas at mahahaba din yung mga patong patong sa mga crests (in my newbie standards mataas na yun) – that was fun! Masaya yung pagbaba sa foot ng Al Naqra which is the tallest sand dune deep in the heart of Sweihan desert, pero mas masaya yung pag-akyat pabalik ng camp site dahil mas challenging, mas may kiliti.   The rookie convoy climbed Al Naqra. Sana maakyat din namin yun someday – o another future goal to ha!At the end of the drive, Mike P. had his recovery tools checked by Mako – congrats in advance bro!One pro tip from Reswee during tire re-inflation: to prolong the life of your compressor, inflate to 25 psi then fully inflate at the petrol station na lang.Lessons learned:1. Repetition, more practice, and more sand time2. Use gravity to your advantage to get around some difficult situations like steep climbs3. Momentum, momentum, momentum4. A psi of tire pressure makes a difference5. Better not fight gravity or risk rolling over6. When sands are severely disturbed already making climbs tougher, deviate from the track a little for a more solid footing. Coordinate with the convoy support for safety.Maraming salamat Team Compass. Marshals Mako & Reswee, thank you bros!Nabigyan na naman ako ng konting push to get through the week dahil may next drive nanaman to look forward to.

							Marshal: JosephDate: 21 -22 February 2019Support team - Our goal is to shows our moral support and enjoys the rest of the company of COMPASS…Thursday night….A long drive to Liwa meeting point where 3 hours’ drive from Dubai… it was cold night and drove alone to the meeting point to join the Dark Skies Extreme Compass team. Everyone end up meeting ADNOC in Abu Al Abayad, filling up petrol and snacks. We decided to proceed in tADNOC meeting point for last gas up. After almost a 1-hour drive, last and quick stop for petrol and rest room to then headed to camp. Arriving Dark Skies Extreme camp… no deflation headed to camp, good thing that it was just a short off-road drive. Compass arrived with all lights on and with loud music courtesy of RedMako sound system. We were welcomed by silence as most of the teams were already in their tents and asleep at midnight. Randy checked for sweet spot for Compass camp, it was chilling night and bonfire was very comforting. Team compass had their briefing and strategized for the challenge, shared map points. We had grilled dry squids for snack under a good night sky and good chat. We decided to hit the sacks at 3am for an early morning wake call at 6:30am.    Friday…. Cold morning, one by one people were waking up and by 7am everyone was wake, preparing for a full day of drive. High spirited, excited and ready for a challenge drive. Compass team had their final briefing with organizers, they were given a track with need to follow the track with short distance will be the champion regardless the time as long the team reached end time at 3pm on Saturday.Deflated tires, Joseph and I hit the dunes to meet compass team at starting point. The team started to cook rice, eggs and corned beef for breakfast but no time to eat as it was time to dispatch the team. We decided to meeting the compass team to point A, first way point.Support team planned to drive until the Liwa truck, participant’s 2nd day camp site. We had a good drive to going to point A, high dune and challenging track. I tried to drive on 4 hi but with soft sands, it was not enough to able to reach the high dune. Got stuck as I stopped to shift to 4 Lo but managed to do self-recovery with left foot braking.  With good pace, we managed to meet the team Compass in point A to handed the cooking wares. The team started their journey challenge from point A to G while we continue to our drive plan to Liwa truck.  It was a good challenging drive and can’t under estimate Liwa’s dunes. The drive was technical, the right speed and distant were needed with this kind of drive. Definitely, off-road driving experience helped a lot to approach the kind of dunes and sands. Soft sand made it difficult for me, I had several second tries but it was fine as our first concern is safety at all times. It was time to check tires pressure, starting driving at 9psi, it increased to 11.5psi. I dropped the tire pressure to 7psi to meet the kind of sand. Passing high dunes, we finally reached the Liwa truck, completing the first leg of our drive plan.  Seeing the truck in the middle of the desert, will make you think how on earth this lorry managed to reach this point. Anyways, it was a good a way point to have a break. We left our mark by sticking Compass sticker representing each Compass member.Since we’re only two Jeeps convoy, Marshal JC allowed me to lead back to practice my driving skills. It was interesting to lead knowing that I don’t need to worry on the convoy having a marshal behind. My goal to find the safest path to on the way out about 25 kms away, completing our drive plan. we approached dunes on straight crest but can’t be avoided the high dunes of Liwa. Finally reached the exit point on time at 2pm and safe… ready to say bye to Liwa dunes until next time.   Distress call… Fast drive…On our way to exit, Marshal JC received a phone call that that team compass didn’t have lunch yet , actually RedBeast broke down after completing almost two (B) points from scheduled 7 point on first challenge day. No lunch and no petrol, we’re heading back to point B to meet team Compass and assess support required. Fast drive with less time to assess high dunes, sometime the other side of the dune is high wall downward. Nearing the location, the exact coordinate was given to us and finally located the team compass.Under RedBeast, Randy and Reswee were opening the differential, the spider gears are broken, ring and pinions heavily chipped. I thought support team will assist RedBeast on the way out while other team will proceed with the challenge. With strong determination, Randy decided to complete the course without 4-wheel drive.  RedBeast was settled and ready to roll again to complete course. The team lost a lot of time and it needs to complete point B to K before 3pm the following day covering almost 100kms of off road driving.   Support team JC, ensured lunch is ready… we end up with good lunch, dried fish, hot soup and rice were just too good for tiring day and empty stomach.It’s 5pm, time to head back before the night darkness comes while team compass stayed to complete the course. It will be extra challenge to drive in Liwa in darkness, proper distance is vital with fast pace drive catching up before the night fall. Support team was out at 6:30pm, just good time to be out of the desert.  Liwa Dark Skies Extreme 2019 experience is in the books… A story to tell… Lesson learned:•	Stay focus, never under estimate dunes•	Keep Jerry can fill up even knowing that it will not be used.  I know extra petrol is not need with our drive plan but I was wrong, it was needed since we went back for support. keep it for safety... •	With strong determination and right attitude, course can be completed

							COMPASS: Code of Acceptable BehaviorAbout COMPASS    Formed in Autumn 2016, the founders have officiated the club on October 10, 2016 (101016) who agreed organize activities focused on Off-roading, SCUBA Diving, and Trekking and other outdoor activities in UAE, which later extended to cycling activity.    COMPASS stands for “COMmunity of Pinoy Adventure Sports Seekers” dedicated and exclusive for Filipinos who are outdoor enthusiast based in the United Arab Emirates.Coherence for the formed community    COMPASS remains exclusive for Filipino nationalities, with tolerance for those who can fluently speak Tagalog or Philippines dialects. This conformity is not debatable by any formed rank in COMPASS and exclusive decision of the Founders.Objectivity    To deliver a healthy community environment, all members must follow the code of conduct as standard behavior in interacting within the group. All members shall be bound by this Code of Conduct.By-Law    1. Founders decision is above all Marshals and other Committee Heads    2. Committee head will always have consulted, informed and communicated decision making. The committee head will act as facilitator to achieve positive outcome for COMPASS as an entirety. A committee is formed to manage each areas of interest to     ensure that activities are addressed appropriately.Leadership in COMPASS activity    1. Founders do not impose or promote Marshalship but welcomes to those who understand the consequences of Offroading, most importantly to the ones who understand COMPASS RUBRICS.        1.1 COMPASS Marshal is granted to those who are ready, willing, able and capable of a Leadership role.        1.2 Marshals is the highest rank awarded to off-roading activity.        1.3 The official Marshal Rank of COMPASS is in this forum.        1.4 Marshalship in COMPASS is a member’s own volition.        1.5 A Marshal should not solicit for our Offroading activities.        1.6 A Marshal should practice a consulted, informed and communicated decision making with fellow Marshals similar to the roles of committee head.        1.7 A Marshal who have demonstrated a consistent contribution will retain its Rank as they have shown verifiable records as Marshal        1.8 All Marshal should arrange at least 10 drives or support in 1 year. In case a Marshal cannot comply to annual drives.            1.8.1 The Marshal will not be permitted to create or lead a drive as Marshal.            1.8.2 To retain the rank Marshal, the Marshal must consistently support 10 drives organized by an active Marshal.            1.8.3 If the Marshal still shows lack of commitment and/or unable to support a fellow Marshal, the rank will be changed to the Advance Rank.            1.8.4 To reclaim the Marshal rank, 10 consistent support drive is required and 1 lead role before changing Advance Rank to Marshal Rank.Competence and BehaviorMEMBERS TO COMPASS    1. Consider your safety as a priority and safety of others always.    2. Trip reports is the only credit and the highest credit that a member can give to a Marshal, sugar coating do not give good impression to other Marshal or other members.    3. Drive with other Marshal, you will discover different driving styles and avoid favoring a Marshal.    4. Abide by the RUBRICS set by COMPASS at all time.    5. Respect NON acceptance for NO Drive/Event registration    6. Not to use COMPASS logos in media material, paraphernalia or other accessories without a consent from a Marshal.    7. Pay any fees commitment relation to an event, purchase orders, offerings, that member opt to have.    8. Fee commitment should be settled regardless of whether the member attends, cancel without notice or last minute cancellation. If you want it, commit.    9. No members should solicit for personal gain.    10. Not to use COMPASS discount privilege to UAE shops without consent from any Marshals or Committee Head.MEMBER TO COMPASS ACTIVITY    1. The official decision of any activity is with the organizing Marshal or Committee Head.    2. Follow the schedule posted by Marshal/Committee Head.    3. Register to the activity if only when you are ready to commit, these are not limited to offroading, dive, trek, cycling or other outdoor and social activities.    4. In an event that you need to cancel your registration or excuse yourself from an event, provide enough time to notify Marshal or the Committee Head    5. For limited slots drives, member who have accumulated more drive within their ranks but do not wish to progress further should give way to members who wishes to progress in the ranks.    6. Passenger should be registered by the member on official drive to have a proper head count, this is not limited to spouse, children, relatives, or friends.    7. If you are leading a private drive avoid wearing COMPASS shirt as this creates a confusion on social media reflected as pretentious Marshal. Use of COMPASS Flag is acceptable as flag is a required safety gear.    8. Member should not drive under the influence of illegal substance.    9. Should follow UAE sanctions on events, uncontrolled events, pandemic/global crisis or any governmental rules that apply to the activity.MEMBER TO MEMBER    1. Be considerate with other members.    2. Everyone is entitled for their opinion but not all should be impulsively reacting to someone’s point.    3. Treat members, families of members with respect and courtesy at all time.    4. Do not physically or verbally harass others.    5. Be cautious with private jokes.    6. No shaming or discriminating other members.    7. Honor commitment agreed between members.MEMBER TO MARSHAL    1. Respect time and effort made by the Marshals, as they are practically spending their family time with you.    2. In a scenario that you have grievance with other members affecting COMPASS. Report any inappropriate behavior of a member to the Marshals and Committee head for action and follow up.    3. In a scenario that you have a grievance with a Marshal, approach another Marshal and Committee heads to raise your concern.    SOCIAL MEDIA & GROUP CHAT    1. Be patient with others – our reading is affected by our mood.    2. Do not give out private and sensitive information.    3. Avoid conversation that will lead to an argument.    4. Avoid private conversations    5. Stay on topic    6. Never leave a group unexpectedly

From Abu Dhabi, we all headed to Hameem were we did our refill of petrol, for my records this is going to be my 1st refuel after leaving home having a full tank of gas. We drove towards our camping site, the terrain was like immediate big climbs, egg baskets and very soft sands. We were not able to reach the planned camp site so we settled to a nearby location where we can reach easily the LIWA wall following morning. Day breaks and woke up to a very scenic view, it was massive and very overwhelming site to see, we took a quick breakfast then afterwards Reswee, Mako and I headed to LIWA wall. Rest of the drivers stayed for a while and drove to another location. Followed Reswee’s track towards LIWA wall, and before reaching there is a big drop which is very steep, did not realize that it was the LIWA wall till I see it from below. Looking at the LIWA Wall for the the 1st time from below, the initial feel was fear and excitement. Accomplishing the King Of the Hill (KOH), it made me understand the deeper side of it when you reach to this level, I know most people will not understand and very few will be able to truly agree. Along with the Compass Rubrics and mindset I do have my own understanding why WE(Compass) have this rubrics, while most of us believe that honing off-road driving skills is main thing Compass is giving you, for me its building your character, bringing out the best in you. To be mentally and skillfully capable both in off-roading and in life. With this I do feel compass might not be for everyone, but it will be a challenge to take if you look at it in a different view or angle I guarantee you that after all the hardships and sacrifices with the proper mindset and goal in your mind no doubt you will achieve. 2 most difficult part of the climb was hitting the foot hill with all you got and making sure you figured out your shifting in your heart. After Completing the KOH, rest of the drivers came and play, we decided to open kuya Pits King’s awning to rest and take our lunch. It was great times, after we had our lunch we accompanied kuya Pits towards exit points, as he was having gearbox problem. We headed to buggy dunes, about 34.7 kms straight shot from where we left Kuya Pits. The drive was smooth having great view and scenery until the last 5-4Kms, it was challenging enough that I had to use 4Lo in some tricky maneuvers. I feel that steering is pulling to the right(psi is 0 im running flat), I hear some weird grinding sound when I use 4H not to mention trying to control my FJ in sharp turn and sudden ditches not to pop out my tires. We reached buggy dunes, set up camp sat and stare at the magnificent view from a far. We had a great dinner prepared by Don and Raymond, Raymond which is my passenger for some duration as Don’s JK is overheating. Following day drove towards moreeb dune, it was a smooth drive, switch 2H to 4H in between sabka’s. 							Few minutes before 6am, Len and I have arrived at the starting point located in Maleha and immediately deflated our tires and setup the flag before anything else then Marshal Mako came and did the same.  After that, we had a chitchat over coffee while waiting for Marshal Bryan & Marshal Reswee but we have started minutes later when Mako received an information that we'll meet them both at the end of Al Faya.1st Leg with Marshal Mako: I'll call it "The Climb"We started with easy ups and downs until we have reached the off-roaders' significant spot to climb in fossil rock that put my Xplorer to a real test.  My team mate, Len had only few attempts with his jeep before he could climb but I was already struggling when there were other off-roaders attempted to climb as well that made the trail more difficult to tackle.  It was on my mind that those 3-car convoy with upgraded suspension jeep, fj and prado couldn't even climb and what more with my stock explorer despite I and those have deflated further more...mine was 10psi but I was motivated when Marshal Mako told me that it was a Compass trail and that no one can climb there but Compass.  My team mate, Len was also reminding me of the tricks on the radio.  That was the only track that has no stones but was already broken and even got more difficult to climb so after 13 attempts I made it 8psi and deviated to rough stony paths and I made it to the top.  Indeed, I left them and proved to be Compass, they took the easy way right after.We connected with Marshal Mako and drove the side of the mountain maintaining the momentum because when you make a mistake it was a hell of attempts once again then we have gone through huge ups and downs.  He was letting his jeep crawl on a straight crest so I had to adjust a little more space between us so I can maintain my momentum until we have reached Red Mako's drop / devil's plunge of Al Faya.  We climbed from the back in the balcony then down in zigzag at the front then immediately climbed back, I never thought i could climb because it's the most difficult side of the hill but surprisingly my explorer did with no second try.It was challenging part of the exam but I have managed without getting stuck.2nd Leg with Marshal Bryan: "The Rush"Al Faya to 2nd December cafeteria, second position to the marshal, we immediately started on a fast pace and I could keep up until i made a mistake when I was too close that made me lost a chance to correct his maneuver into safer for my car.  We passed a double ditch that made the explorer jump and dived on to the second one that resulted to the disconnection of hose from the radiator due to impact pressure, I thought that I broke my radiator otherwise I would have been disqualified.  Luckily it was just minor and I always bring 1.5Lx6 water for any emergency apart from my 500mlx12 water to drink so that made me back on track but with the burden in mind that it might overheat anytime.  However, it was a reminder of making a distance but I have managed to cope up the test without overheating and as well not to get stuck until the end of Marshal Bryan's part. I can't remember the details of this part because I was in a state of shock.  Coolant splash on my windshield swept my memory Hahaha I just drove and completed.  Tricky small dunes and a lot of ditches, bushes and tree branches for a fast pace, distance is the key.3rd Leg with Marshal Reswee: "The Sharp Straight Crest"From the 2nd December cafeteria to Badayer, Marshal Reswee really put our skills to test as well as my explorer.  Like any other game the last part mostly the toughest.As the earth's temperature starts to rise Mai Dubai water barely kept my engine & transmission to their operating temperature to produce their actual power so I always fell on side cresting and do second tries because there's really no more juice to squeeze from my first gear of high gears and if I had to switch low gears it overheats.  On our first couple of kilometers I was surprised and got stuck on a ditch while coming down from a straight crest so I started loosing energy since I couldn't turn on my AC as well to ventilate myself.  We were driving against the wind so side cresting requires sand grip and power, gladly that I was already on 8psi so I could somehow keep up but sometimes I could not predict the exit whether on left or right because Marshal Reswee said he's on 15psi so  when he looses grip he does not go over the dunes sometimes as well and come back down.At the Pink Rock side slope I did few attempts to go over the huge wall and climbing atop between narrow rocks requires second try only the ones I never pass before so we have proceeded to a lot of sharp straight crest that mostly against the wind that made me exhausted due to recoveries when I get stuck and the heat.  I was thankful that my team mate's (Len) car is a JK cannot be easily get stuck on since it's short wheel base vehicle and he posses an awesome skill and with a very kind & generous character so we worry about my explorer only, he never get tired pulling me from stuck. My trick was after he recovers me or I do second try I let him go first so I can calculate and that did not fail me, I didn't go more than second tries on a sharp straight crest.Straight crests that I have never experienced before such as on a tip of a cone but none of those made my mind to deviate the course.  If the car in front of me could go I should go regardless of the vehicle and I was confident that Marshals don't let us go through if we really cannot.3 kilometers away from the finish line, Mai Dubai water could not maintain the explorer's operating temperature and it rises to the top so we had to call for a break to cool the engine down and avoid further mishaps.  We still have a lot of time left (thanks to Marshall Mako for time keeping) so we have freshen ourselves before the last battle.We proceeded to climb Big Red dune, along the way I fell from the balcony so Len followed Marshall Reswee and I did second try but then I got stuck on a corner prior to get to the top.  I tried not to get help from my team mate since he's already few large dunes away from me.  My trusty sand tracks did not let me down and I was out in couple of minutes then I was able to connect to them again until we have completed the examination.It was a battle of self-determination and technical skills, if I had not learn that from the Compass training I would have quit the examination due to heat and barely loose my focus on driving.A big thanks to Compass family especially to Marshal's Reswee, Bryan and Mako, support of Jean and my awesome team mate, Len.I look forward to see the result of passing this exam to proceed to R2.Keep the sands flying!!!

							Newbies Chasing Sunset 06Aug2023Drive Difficulty: NewbieDETAILS:Date: Sunday, 06 August 2023Meeting Time: 3:30 PMMoving Time: 4:00 PMDrive End: Until SunsetMy first time driving in the desert. No experience at all, however, due to our supportive marshals everything went well.Marshals: Marvin, Bryan and ResweeThis are my learnings, experiences and observations on my off-road drive. Before we drive to the desert, Marshals demonstrate how it will go during our drive in desert. Straight Crest, Side Crest, Egg basket, how to control the power and momentum, car distance, proper tyre air pressure etc. Since I was a beginner I don't have any upgrades on my car, we manually remove the fuse bar to switch off the ABS and Traction as it will not be use during the off road drive. I also deflated the tire pressure to 9psi. Fast forward, everything is fine I actually experience everything during my first drive like overshoot, pop out, stuck in the sand. It feels good and terrible all at the same time. Thanks to the Marshals for all the support and guidance. At least I experience it at the early stage of my off road driving so next time I would know how to handle it. Also, as we end up late. It's so difficult to drive as I don't have any auxiliary lights, again, thanks to the marshal for helping me see the track by driving behind me and light up the track. Pointers1. Right amount of power and momentum to avoid overshooting on the straight crest2. Safe distance is very important and know how to read the flag of the car in front of you.3. 90% of the time you wont be using a hard press brake as car will slow down it self when not accelerating since the tire pressure is low.4. When you feel that the car is under power during the side crest its important not to fight the gravity and point the steering to the gravity and go down.5. Do not stop side ways during side cresting.All and all, everything is nice and fun and scary. Hehe

Upon reaching the DP, we met Marshal Reswee and started deflating then. We reached the camp site where the other members camped the night before.We started socializing with other members while Bryan was getting ready for his safety briefing. We introduced ourselves to the group before Bryan began his safety talk. Upon completing the first part of the safety talk focusing on the general discussion and origins of Compass, Marvin and Javee decided to create a separate talk for us Rookies. The Rookie team comprised of MP, myself, Javee and Marvin. As it was my first Rookie drive, Marvin and Javee were very specific in introducing what lies ahead in this drive. They informed of the leveling up of our drives from newbie. The first to expect was there will be a lot of side cresting and switching. The Marshals said that they will assess my side crest and switching maneuvers in a slower pace. Javee created the convoy management as he was the lead, while I followed, the third was MP and finally Marvin as the sweep. We attacked some small dunes with some side crests and switching. They quickly found out some flaws in my switching movements and corrected them. We were getting a hang of it while doing multiple passes on the dunes.When we were getting composed with the drives, we then progressed to higher dunes in the process. There were numerous low and high dunes where we did a lot of side crests and switching. I felt that the level of drives have stepped-up so much from the newbie drives I was used to. I felt more courage and understanding of my car and its capabilities. They informed me of the “pump-pump” throttle control and the “left-right” approach to the dunes. I found that my car’s capability is more enhanced with the Pump-pump and left-right maneuvers. These new techniques do not put strain to the engine as well as the car can crawl up the dune higher than what I used to do! This technique is amazing! 							The drive started with 2 other newbie members in Al Qudra, with MC as the marshall and JP as support. It was around 38-41 deg. Celsius which made deflating the tires a little uncomfortable. Nevertheless, we continued with the drive and it was enjoyable and there was no humidity. I have learned the following;1. The psi of tyres during afternoon drives especially during summer weather should be 12 psi in contrast to the normal 15 psi as the heat would bring the tyre pressure back to 15 psi during the drive. We tested this mid drive and it was true;2. The rpm used by others while climbing dunes are different for each car. I learned this by overshooting in 1 climb as I heard over the radio that 4k rpm is required for the attempt whereas fj required only 2.5 or less;3. The color of the sand is almost the same around your vehicle when the sun is on top of the vehicle making it more difficult to read the sand in the afternoon. It is better to drive with the sun in front of you rather than behind you.

Connect and must go with the convoy and wait until further notice from the lead when the convoy will hold or continues.- Correct timing when up shifting and downshifting when climbing a steep / big dunes.- Safety approach on side crests and straight crests.- PSI check every now and then is very crucialWe completed our intermediate drive with full of lessons and inputs learned from our Lead marshall Bryan and sweep / floating marshall Jc. 							October 11, 2019 – Al Faqaa Lead-JPTeammates:Ronald - Pajero (Rookie)Carl – Ranger Pickup (Newbie)Len – JK (Newbie)Jean – JKU (Rookie)The night before, we celebrated the 3rd Anniversary of our Compass family and during the festivities, the Marshall let us draw our team member. As Founder/Marshal Randy said, we will drive according to our weakest driver. Surely enough, I realized that this was going to be a very challenging experience. The morning came, all teams were briefed and off we went to tackle the GPS challenge. At first going to WP1 and WP2 , my convoy struggled to follow my tracks because of the soft sand, straight crest and drops. Even with my slow pace, My team could barely keep up and follow my tracks. I myself had to be recovered at the very first waypoint because I had to stop to wait for the team in front of me to overcome the dune. After waypoint 2, I realized that I had to change my strategy, I will stick to my tracks, follow a straight line to the waypoints so I had to appoint Ronald to lead and recon tracks for them to follow so they can do it easier and faster. After waypoint 2, everything else went smooth. I had to wait at every waypoint for my team. During the traverse, I can hear in my team’s radio chatter that the pickup truck still had to struggle at some instances and needed to be recovered. Even the JK and JKU had times when they had to be recovered or had to do second tries. Good thing for our radio communication and parts where I had to stop so I can oversee them and give them proper instruction.We finished all waypoint all together. We felt we were the slowest because the marshals said we were the last team to arrive.  But our feeling of achievement and joy in completing the challenge safely was unimaginable.I am proud of my team for what we have accomplished. Carl with the pickup truck, you never gave up. Ronald with the Pajero, your guidance was impeccable. Len with your communication, clear and concise. Tackled medium height dunes and few bowls at this time.Upon reaching it, I radioed Mako to inform him that I reached my first stop and I'll be heading to the next. I have a glimpsed of his lights on my far back and I start to move.The second point is around 2.8 km northwest of my current position.
